figures.caption: Figure 5: One-dimensional reaction–diffusion problem in Example 2 with ε = 10−10. (a) Neural-network approximation uN . An inset provides a zoomed-in view near x = 1 to highlight the boundary-layer structure. (b) Absolute error |u− uN |.

Figure 9: Numerical results for the coupled system problem in Example 6 with ε = 10−10. Panels (a) and (b) show the neural-network approximation u1,N and its corresponding absolute error, respectively, while panels (c) and (d) present the corresponding results for u2,N .

Figure 10: Numerical solution of the two-dimensional Poisson–Boltzmann equation on an irregular domain with ε = 10−10. (a) Neural-network predicted solution, showing boundary layers formed along the entire boundary. (b) Top view of the solution, demonstrating that the solution remains close to zero in the interior region.

Figure 7: Numerical results for Example 4 with ε = 10−10. Panel (a) shows the regular component ur, which remains smooth over the entire domain. Panel (b) displays the singular component us, exhibiting sharp boundarylayer behavior along x = 1 and y = 1. Panel (c) presents the predicted solution uN , and panel (d) shows the corresponding absolute error.

Figure 4: One-dimensional convection–diffusion–reaction problem with ε = 10−10 in Example 1. (a) Learned regular component ur, which remains smooth over the entire domain. (b) Learned singular component us, accurately capturing the boundary layer near the left endpoint while remaining nearly constant elsewhere. (c) Neural-network approximation uN with an zoom-in inset view near the boundary layer at x = 0. (d) Absolute error |u−uN |, showing that the maximum error is localized near the boundary layer and remains of comparable magnitude throughout the domain.

Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the neural network architecture for the one-dimensional case. The network takes three inputs, x, ϕL/ε, and ϕR/ε, which are fed into three separate fully connected blocks with a variable number of hidden layers. The outputs of the hidden layers are subsequently combined and mapped to a single scalar output u.
Figure 8: Two-dimensional reaction–diffusion problem in Example 4 with ε = 10−10. (a) Neural-network approximation uN . (b) Absolute error |u− uN |.

Figure 2: Neural network architecture for the two-dimensional regular domain. Six inputs, x, y, and the scaled level-set functions ϕL/ε, ϕR/ε, ϕB/ε, and ϕT /ε, are processed by seven fully connected blocks to realize the solution decomposition, and the final output u is obtained through algebraic combinations of the block outputs.

Figure 6: Numerical results for the coupled system problem in Example 3 with ε = 10−10. Panels (a) and (b) show the neural-network approximation u1,N and its corresponding absolute error, respectively, while panels (c) and (d) present the corresponding results for u2,N . Both solution components exhibit non-monotonic boundary-layer structures near the left endpoint x = 0 (see the insets in panels (a) and (c) with the zoomed-in views near the left endpoint x = 0), which are accurately resolved by the proposed method. The absolute errors remain at the level of 10−7–10−8 throughout the domain.
